In this lesson, we're going to talk about finalizing your initial offer that you'll send to the seller. But before we start, I want to take a minute to talk about Grant and his recent appearance on undercover billionaire on Discovery. If you watched the show, you might remember that throughout, he started and built a new company called Wake Up Pueblo. By the end of the series, Grant and his business partner successfully brought in $490,000 worth of contracts from various customers after only one month in business. A private business evaluator was then hired in the final episode of the series to come in and assess the business. In the end, Wake Up Pueblo was valued at $5.5 million. It was an incredible accomplishment for the entire team. I tell you this story for one reason, to highlight the necessity and value of a trained and experienced business evaluator. As I said in the previous lesson on business valuation, you want to work with professionals on this process. It's critical you have a professional to help you evaluate the value of the business you're trying to purchase. You then take that valuation and with the help of an accountant, you identify all your opportunities for funding and structuring the best offer to fit the deal type you've selected. Now with that said, you should get comfortable with the numbers and the financials used to structure your offer. We've created a very simple sheet for you to fill in using the past three years of financial statements the sellers should have provided you. You can find a detailed walkthrough in the resource section of this module. This will show you exactly how to fill it out and what each cell on the table represents. You take this information and with the help of your accountant, you can plug and play with different financing options, add and adjust deferred payments to the current owner and evaluate what your annual debt repayment on any loans will look like for each particular year. How you use this sheet ultimately depends on the deal type you've decided on. Or it will tell you what deal type you need to go to. Finally, in the very last cell of the table, you will see debt coverage. This is simply taking the total annual estimated cost you will owe to repay any loans or deferred seller payments and divide that by the EBITDA at the very top of the table. This gives you some idea of how well you can expect to cover the annual loan costs using the business expected future income. We advise that EBITDA should never be below one and a half times the annual cost of debt repayment. If your EBITDA for example would be a million dollars to use a round number, you'd want to cap your annual debt payments around 650,000 dollars because that would leave you as a business owner 350,000 dollars left over. After working with your accountant, you want to have two deal scenarios. Your ideal offer and your walk away offer. This will provide you with all the tools and guidance you and your professional will need to move to the next step. Preparing yourself for a progression of offers and alternatives that can mix and match in the negotiation process in order to close the deal. We've outlined six deal adjustments that you can make throughout the negotiation process to get the deal closed. Your accountant and you might dream up others, but these are a starting point to get you and your creative deal making juices flowing. The first offer you make should always be slightly lower than your ideal offer you've already decided on. Then you can incrementally step up that offer, but be careful not to dip below one and a half times your EBITDA in annual debts. It will just put too much pressure on your business especially if you're a new buyer. The whole principle of offer progressions is that we've predetermined a chain of offers and counter offers that we're comfortable with and can live with. This upfront planning not only allows you to respond to a seller in a timely manner, but it also ensures you don't let your emotions lead to bad negotiations. You've done the hard work ahead of time to outline multiple acceptable offers and you know your walk away point. In total we recommend outlining at least six deal alternatives. Deal number one that you submit. Maybe it's a 25% reduction in closing payments or purchase price that you're comfortable Deal number two 100% of the ideal closing payment or purchase price if you get a counter from the seller. Deal number three offering an additional year or more of seller financing. Deal number four changing offer to less than 100% of deal equity and maybe having the business owner carry an active interest in the business and recap them later when the business is performing better. Deal number five offering earn out so if the business performs you'll pay more. Deal number six sell equity to an external investor to raise additional funds. Although if you can't meet your debt to equity ratios do you really want to give up more equity in your business and pay a third party more. These are all things you're going to have to think about. Talk to your professional about. There's another dozen things I could rattle off in ways to make deals. How you structure the deal. How you tax advantage the deal for the seller. You know there's just so many different ways to go about it but the point here is this is where having a great professional is very important to help you through this process. Now in the resources section of this module you'll find a worksheet that gives you further guidance and easy to follow instructions on outlining these six scenarios. But remember that every business is unique and every deal is unique. Try to think creatively and outside the box during negotiations. These are simply only jumping off points. I'd like to spend our time today ensuring you understand the fundamental function of the sequencing process and how it is likely to play out between you and a potential seller. To start know that these deals you outlined are not set in stone. Each incremental step in the offer builds on top of each other in combination or you can mix and match the different parts to create a specific offer for your deal. Again offer one is usually anchored lower than your ideal offer. This gives us a good jumping off point to incrementally increase and adjust from there. If we started with your ideal offer we'd only be working up from that point and it'll push the price higher. With that said if you find a unicorn business with a seller who's willing to move forward you may not want to screw around with them because once they get the idea they might sell they may get open to entertaining other offers so speed and the ability to move quickly is equally important. It's almost unheard of for a business owner not to counter at least once or twice in the buying selling process. From my experience it's not uncommon for you and the seller to be going back and forth on the offer every three or four days. You might also not hear back from them at all for an initial period of time. You should not give up. We actually recommend waiting and sending the next offer in your sequence. Now it's important to note that there's an art to countering. You don't want to respond immediately to sellers. You want to acknowledge that they've responded and then give them some time for you to think about it and come back with a counter offer. If you respond too quickly they may perceive it as an eagerness and even push the price higher. We cover even more of these types of tips and tricks in the negotiation section later in this module. Having your offer progression completely mapped out before you make any offer whatsoever puts you in a strong negotiation position. You'll be in the driver's seat for this deal. What you'll find with highly motivated sellers is that the first three offers that you generate in your progression are going to be more than enough to get a deal done. The fourth, fifth and sixth offer you might never have to make those offers or even address them. But having those numbers and having those structures in your pocket will be extremely valuable to you to have the confidence to move forward. And you'll also be able to outline your walk away points if this deal gets out of hand which will protect you and make sure you and your professional are on the same page should you get emotionally attached to a deal. Coming up next we'll show you the offer letter template that you can drop these numbers in and make your offers. As I mentioned before there'll be a lot more detailed training on earnouts and seller financing and all the different ways of raising finances in the next module. I'll see you in the next lesson and until then, find some deals.
